clusters.title: Ultra-processed foods damage your focus even if you eat healthily
clusters.summary: New research from Monash University, the University of São Paulo and Deakin University shows that a diet high in heavily processed foods can negatively impact the brain's ability to focus and increases the risk of developing dementia. The study published in Alzheimer's & Dementia: Diagnosis, Assessment & Disease Monitoring, examined the diets and cognitive health of more than 2,100 Australian dementia-free adults middle-aged and older.

items.title: Ultra-processed foods damage your focus even if you eat healthily
items.description: New research from Monash University, the University of São Paulo and Deakin University shows that a diet high in heavily processed foods can negatively impact the brain's ability to focus and increases the risk of developing dementia. The study published in Alzheimer's & Dementia: Diagnosis, Assessment & Disease Monitoring, examined the diets and cognitive health of more than 2,100 Australian dementia-free adults middle-aged and older.

clusters.title: Psychology textbooks still misrepresent famous experiments and controversial debates
clusters.summary: College psychology textbooks still struggle to accurately present controversial topics and historical events, according to new research. While some improvements have occurred, textbooks frequently repeat scientific urban legends.

items.title: Psychology textbooks still misrepresent famous experiments and controversial debates
items.description: College psychology textbooks still struggle to accurately present controversial topics and historical events, according to new research. While some improvements have occurred, textbooks frequently repeat scientific urban legends.

clusters.title: New study suggests dreams function as a “multimotive simulation space”
clusters.summary: New research indicates that dreams often act as a simulation for social hurdles. By analyzing hundreds of dream reports, scientists found that themes like status and self-protection frequently organize the content of our sleep narratives.

items.title: New study suggests dreams function as a “multimotive simulation space”
items.description: New research indicates that dreams often act as a simulation for social hurdles. By analyzing hundreds of dream reports, scientists found that themes like status and self-protection frequently organize the content of our sleep narratives.

clusters.title: Scientists discover hidden gut trigger behind ALS and dementia
clusters.summary: A new study reveals that gut bacteria may play a key role in triggering ALS and frontotemporal dementia. Harmful sugars produced by these microbes can spark immune responses that damage the brain. This breakthrough explains why some genetically at-risk people develop the diseases while others don’t.

items.title: Scientists discover hidden gut trigger behind ALS and dementia
items.description: A new study reveals that gut bacteria may play a key role in triggering ALS and frontotemporal dementia. Harmful sugars produced by these microbes can spark immune responses that damage the brain. This breakthrough explains why some genetically at-risk people develop the diseases while others don’t. Even more promising, reducing these sugars improved brain health in experiments, hinting at new treatment possibilities.

clusters.title: Dragonflies can see a color humans can’t and it could change medicine
clusters.summary: Dragonflies may see the world in a way that pushes beyond human limits—and surprisingly, they do it using the same molecular trick we evolved ourselves. Scientists discovered that these insects can detect extremely deep red light, even edging into near-infrared, thanks to a specialized visual protein strikingly similar to the one in human eyes. This ability likely helps them spot mates mid-flight by picking up subtle differences in reflected light.

items.title: Dragonflies can see a color humans can’t and it could change medicine
items.description: Dragonflies may see the world in a way that pushes beyond human limits—and surprisingly, they do it using the same molecular trick we evolved ourselves. Scientists discovered that these insects can detect extremely deep red light, even edging into near-infrared, thanks to a specialized visual protein strikingly similar to the one in human eyes. This ability likely helps them spot mates mid-flight by picking up subtle differences in reflected light.

clusters.title: ADHD brains show sleep-like activity even while awake
clusters.summary: Researchers have identified a surprising brain pattern that may help explain why people with ADHD often struggle to stay focused. Even while awake, their brains can slip into brief episodes of “sleep-like” activity during demanding tasks. These moments are linked to more mistakes, slower reaction times, and lapses in attention.

items.title: ADHD brains show sleep-like activity even while awake
items.description: Researchers have identified a surprising brain pattern that may help explain why people with ADHD often struggle to stay focused. Even while awake, their brains can slip into brief episodes of “sleep-like” activity during demanding tasks. These moments are linked to more mistakes, slower reaction times, and lapses in attention.

clusters.title: Scientists discover “overflow valve” in cells linked to Parkinson’s Disease
clusters.summary: Researchers have identified a crucial ion channel, TMEM175, that acts like an overflow valve in the cell’s recycling system. It regulates acidity inside lysosomes, ensuring they function properly. When TMEM175 is faulty, toxic buildup can occur, contributing to diseases like Parkinson’s.

items.title: Scientists discover “overflow valve” in cells linked to Parkinson’s Disease
items.description: Researchers have identified a crucial ion channel, TMEM175, that acts like an overflow valve in the cell’s recycling system. It regulates acidity inside lysosomes, ensuring they function properly. When TMEM175 is faulty, toxic buildup can occur, contributing to diseases like Parkinson’s. This breakthrough reveals a promising new target for future therapies.

clusters.title: Early adaptive skills may shield children's brains after exposure to disaster-related prenatal stress
clusters.summary: Researchers from the City University of New York (CUNY) Graduate Center and Queens College suggest that building strong adaptive skills in early childhood may serve as a buffer against the detrimental effects of prenatal stress on a child's developing brain.

items.title: Early adaptive skills may shield children's brains after exposure to disaster-related prenatal stress
items.description: Researchers from the City University of New York (CUNY) Graduate Center and Queens College suggest that building strong adaptive skills in early childhood may serve as a buffer against the detrimental effects of prenatal stress on a child's developing brain.

clusters.title: Two common drugs may reverse fatty liver disease, study finds
clusters.summary: Scientists have discovered that combining two existing drugs can dramatically reduce liver fat linked to a common and often silent disease. The treatment not only improved liver health in animal models but also showed potential to lower heart-related risks. Interestingly, using lower doses of both drugs together worked just as well as higher doses alone.

items.title: Two common drugs may reverse fatty liver disease, study finds
items.description: Scientists have discovered that combining two existing drugs can dramatically reduce liver fat linked to a common and often silent disease. The treatment not only improved liver health in animal models but also showed potential to lower heart-related risks. Interestingly, using lower doses of both drugs together worked just as well as higher doses alone. While promising, the findings still need to be tested in humans.

clusters.title: Feeling angry makes people more likely to share news from low-credibility sources
clusters.summary: A new study reveals that feeling morally angry makes you more likely to share misinformation online. Researchers found that anger causes people to act impulsively and ignore source credibility, fueling the rapid spread of fake news.

items.title: Feeling angry makes people more likely to share news from low-credibility sources
items.description: A new study reveals that feeling morally angry makes you more likely to share misinformation online. Researchers found that anger causes people to act impulsively and ignore source credibility, fueling the rapid spread of fake news.

clusters.title: This simple fatty acid could restore failing vision
clusters.summary: Scientists at UC Irvine have found a way to potentially reverse age-related vision loss by targeting the ELOVL2 “aging gene” and restoring vital fatty acids in the retina. Their experiments in mice show that supplementing with specific polyunsaturated fatty acids—not just DHA—can restore visual function and even reverse cellular aging signs.

items.title: This simple fatty acid could restore failing vision
items.description: Scientists at UC Irvine have found a way to potentially reverse age-related vision loss by targeting the ELOVL2 “aging gene” and restoring vital fatty acids in the retina. Their experiments in mice show that supplementing with specific polyunsaturated fatty acids—not just DHA—can restore visual function and even reverse cellular aging signs.

clusters.title: Everyday infections, not vaccines, are linked to an increased risk of childhood stroke
clusters.summary: While strokes in children remain incredibly rare, new research shows that experiencing a recent infection can temporarily double the risk. Scientists confirmed that standard childhood vaccinations do not trigger these vascular events.

items.title: Everyday infections, not vaccines, are linked to an increased risk of childhood stroke
items.description: While strokes in children remain incredibly rare, new research shows that experiencing a recent infection can temporarily double the risk. Scientists confirmed that standard childhood vaccinations do not trigger these vascular events.

clusters.title: Early weight gain is linked to lifelong health consequences
clusters.summary: Putting on weight earlier in life may be more dangerous than previously thought. Researchers found that early adulthood obesity significantly raises the risk of premature death, especially from major diseases like heart disease and diabetes. The longer the body carries excess weight, the greater the damage appears to be.

items.title: Early weight gain is linked to lifelong health consequences
items.description: Putting on weight earlier in life may be more dangerous than previously thought. Researchers found that early adulthood obesity significantly raises the risk of premature death, especially from major diseases like heart disease and diabetes. The longer the body carries excess weight, the greater the damage appears to be. Interestingly, cancer risk in women didn’t follow this pattern, suggesting other biological factors are at play.

clusters.title: This simple 3-amino acid trick boosts mRNA therapy 20-fold
clusters.summary: A trio of common amino acids may hold the key to unlocking far more powerful gene therapies. Researchers found that adding them to lipid nanoparticles can boost mRNA delivery up to 20-fold and push CRISPR editing efficiency close to 90%. The trick isn’t changing the drug—but helping cells take it in more easily.

items.title: This simple 3-amino acid trick boosts mRNA therapy 20-fold
items.description: A trio of common amino acids may hold the key to unlocking far more powerful gene therapies. Researchers found that adding them to lipid nanoparticles can boost mRNA delivery up to 20-fold and push CRISPR editing efficiency close to 90%. The trick isn’t changing the drug—but helping cells take it in more easily. In early tests, the approach dramatically improved survival and treatment outcomes, pointing to a simple but game-changing upgrade for future medicine.

clusters.title: Mechanical forces from the beating heart may help prevent cancer cell growth
clusters.summary: Scientists may have discovered another way the human body tries to protect itself from cancer. New research on mice suggests that the heart's constant beating may prevent tumor growth in cardiac tissue. Most organs are vulnerable to cancer, but the heart is something of an anomaly.

items.title: Mechanical forces from the beating heart may help prevent cancer cell growth
items.description: Scientists may have discovered another way the human body tries to protect itself from cancer. New research on mice suggests that the heart's constant beating may prevent tumor growth in cardiac tissue. Most organs are vulnerable to cancer, but the heart is something of an anomaly. While cancer can spread from other parts of the body to the heart, tumors rarely start there. It's a medical mystery that has puzzled scientists for years.

clusters.title: Huge study finds no evidence cannabis helps anxiety, depression, or PTSD
clusters.summary: The largest review of medicinal cannabis to date found it doesn’t effectively treat anxiety, depression, or PTSD—despite millions using it for those reasons. Researchers warn it could even make mental health worse, raising risks like psychosis and addiction while delaying proven treatments. Some limited benefits were seen for conditions like insomnia and autism, but the evidence is weak.

items.title: Huge study finds no evidence cannabis helps anxiety, depression, or PTSD
items.description: The largest review of medicinal cannabis to date found it doesn’t effectively treat anxiety, depression, or PTSD—despite millions using it for those reasons. Researchers warn it could even make mental health worse, raising risks like psychosis and addiction while delaying proven treatments. Some limited benefits were seen for conditions like insomnia and autism, but the evidence is weak. The findings are fueling calls for stricter oversight as cannabis use continues to rise.

clusters.title: Study finds children in the US die at higher rates than peers in other high-income nations
clusters.summary: At every age, children in the United States die at higher rates than peers in other high-income nations, according to a new study from Children's Hospital of Philadelphia. Findings from the study were presented during the Pediatric Academic Societies (PAS) 2026 Meeting, taking place April 24–27 in Boston.

items.title: Study finds children in the US die at higher rates than peers in other high-income nations
items.description: At every age, children in the United States die at higher rates than peers in other high-income nations, according to a new study from Children's Hospital of Philadelphia. Findings from the study were presented during the Pediatric Academic Societies (PAS) 2026 Meeting, taking place April 24–27 in Boston.

clusters.title: Large international study confirms similar efficacy and safety of common fluid treatments for pediatric sepsis
clusters.summary: A major study, led by researchers at Children's Hospital of Philadelphia, Nemours Children's Health, and Children's National Hospital and involving an extensive network of medical centers across the United States and abroad, found that different types of crystalloid fluid resuscitation were equally effective for staving off the most serious adverse kidney events after the treatment of pediatric patients with suspected septic shock.

items.title: Large international study confirms similar efficacy and safety of common fluid treatments for pediatric sepsis
items.description: A major study, led by researchers at Children's Hospital of Philadelphia, Nemours Children's Health, and Children's National Hospital and involving an extensive network of medical centers across the United States and abroad, found that different types of crystalloid fluid resuscitation were equally effective for staving off the most serious adverse kidney events after the treatment of pediatric patients with suspected septic shock.

clusters.title: Eating more fruits and vegetables tied to unexpected lung cancer risk
clusters.summary: A surprising new study suggests that eating a very healthy diet—packed with fruits, vegetables, and whole grains—might be linked to a higher risk of lung cancer in younger non-smokers. Researchers found that patients under 50 diagnosed with lung cancer often had better-than-average diets, raising the possibility that pesticide exposure from conventionally grown produce could be a hidden culprit.

items.title: Eating more fruits and vegetables tied to unexpected lung cancer risk
items.description: A surprising new study suggests that eating a very healthy diet—packed with fruits, vegetables, and whole grains—might be linked to a higher risk of lung cancer in younger non-smokers. Researchers found that patients under 50 diagnosed with lung cancer often had better-than-average diets, raising the possibility that pesticide exposure from conventionally grown produce could be a hidden culprit.

clusters.title: This common gout drug may slash heart attack and stroke risk
clusters.summary: A major new study reveals that treating gout may do far more than ease painful joint flare-ups—it could also protect the heart. Researchers found that patients who took common gout medications like allopurinol and successfully lowered their blood urate levels had a significantly reduced risk of heart attacks, strokes, and cardiovascular death over five years.
